entity.summary: Short-lived centralized digital-asset exchange that launched in January 2019 with cryptocurrency and tokenized-stock trading, later expanded into security tokens, and stopped deposits and trading in November 2019 amid financial difficulties and bankruptcy proceedings involving its operating company.
entity.notes: DX.Exchange presented itself as Estonia-based, while reporting and employee proceedings identified Israeli company CX Technologies as the operating company. The November 3 service halt is used as the death date; the October 24 employee petition concerning CX Technologies is recorded separately.

events.title: DX.Exchange launched
events.description: DX.Exchange opened with cryptocurrency and fiat trading and tokenized representations of shares in major public companies.
events.notes: The January 7 launch date was announced by DX.Exchange and independently reported before launch.

events.title: Security-token trading introduced
events.description: DX.Exchange announced security-token trading and security-token-offering listings after its initial cryptocurrency and tokenized-stock launch.
events.notes: Recorded as a material product expansion rather than a second entity launch.

events.title: Employees petitioned to wind up the operating company
events.description: Employees of CX Technologies, identified in reporting as the company operating DX.Exchange, filed an Israeli court petition alleging unpaid wages and seeking to wind up the company.
events.notes: The petition concerned CX Technologies rather than the Estonia-registered exchange entity presented on the website; the relationship is therefore described conservatively.

events.title: Deposits and trading stopped
events.description: DX.Exchange stopped accepting deposits, suspended trading, cancelled open orders, and sought a merger or sale after stating that its security, support, and technology costs were not economically feasible on its own.
events.notes: Although the announcement described the closure as temporary, no verified restart followed and bankruptcy proceedings involving the operating company were reported later that month.

evidence.title: DX.Exchange and Fingenom Group Announce Strategic Partnership
evidence.notes: Company-issued release stating that the exchange was scheduled to go live on January 7, 2019.

evidence.title: Token Exchange to Enable Trading of Nasdaq-Listed Companies
evidence.notes: Independent pre-launch report confirming the January 7 launch plan and initial tokenized-stock and crypto-trading scope.

evidence.title: Nasdaq-Powered EU Digital Exchange DX Launches Security Token Trading
evidence.notes: Independent report on the March 2019 security-token product expansion and the DXtech Exchange name.

evidence.title: Israeli Firm Behind DX.Exchange Goes Bankrupt Following Staff Petition
evidence.notes: Reports the October 24 employee petition and identifies CX Technologies as the company operating DX.Exchange.

evidence.title: DX.Exchange board closure notice
evidence.notes: Original company notice URL preserved through archive lookup; the live blog is no longer relied upon as a safe access path.

evidence.title: DX.Exchange Halts Operations, Seeks Buyer 10 Months After Launch
evidence.notes: Independent report confirming suspended deposits, the withdrawal deadline, and the possibility that operations would not resume.

evidence.title: Israeli crypto exchange DX halts activity
evidence.notes: Independent business reporting describing financial difficulties and stating that the exchange would not resume without an acquisition.
